speaker: interviewer
text: What church did you belong to in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we belong-- I was baptized in the St. John, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Warsaw. That's cathedral. And that was the place where all out Polish king, who lived in Warsaw, go during the-- special corridor to the cathedral for mass, the St. John Cathedral.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell us, as you remember, what relationships your father had, in the '30s, with Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father has good relationship and special with couple family. Because we cannot talk generally, because, you know, you have contact with some business people, you know. You stick with this people if you trust and vice versa.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was-- has good relationship with a family, Melamed and Birnbaum and Landau, a couple of families, you know? And because this was a good relationship, for example, 1930-- 1940, if I remember-- '40, '41, I was with my father on the bat-- bar mitzvah to our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] friends' family, Mr. Melamed.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was two boys, at the same time, going through the ceremony. And right now, one of the boys, maybe 65 years old, is in-- [LAUGHS] in Tel Aviv. And we have later, there, I show you the photo, Mr. Melamed and the one Melamed son still in life. He's in the Tel Aviv. The other one, brother, died during the war with Arab. I think so. This is the '60-something. And that's the part. Now, my-- because we was good connected with the people during the war, 1939 and after 1940, I was-- I come back from the army, because I was captured, 17 September, by Soviet Army, on the east. I come back to Warsaw. And I work for some transport company. And later there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after 1940, I was connected with the-- like, I sell good, what was produced in this area, where was ghetto, in Aryan part, for example, the toys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working for grand-- really, for father of Mrs. Melamed. I was representative there. I took the-- how you calls? What this-- that one store need, I put on the paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later there, was sent, to disguise, to the store, from the ghetto, from the Birnbaum Company. And I collect money. And I bring, after a couple of days, to Mr. Birnbaum. That was my connection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get, specifically, into that, I'd like to go back, for a few minutes, to talk about the business relationship, before the beginning of the war, that your father may or may not have had with Jewish people. Could you describe that to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Please tell again.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- before the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --could you describe the business relationship that your father had with Jewish people, if any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure we have-- we know Melamed much before 1936. And we was-- not we. My father was involved in this business and was very close with Melamed. And we know each other before the war not after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] buy from Mrs. Melamed different part of the machinery or goods and something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a business was your father in, in the '30s, that required him to go to Mrs. Melamed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Galanterii, like, for example, the knitting stuff and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] everything what was knitted. And Mr. Melamed, this time, has special privilege for some couple big factories in Poland and has the like wholesale-- the representative. And my father bought from there, the-- from Mr. Melamed. That was beginning of the business. That's 1936 or something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father's business located?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, on Mariensztat Street, very close to the old town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were--

speaker: interviewee
text: 23 Mariensztat Street, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that near where the ghetto was built?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Is not far from the ghetto and still not-- not too close. Not-- and maybe five miles, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have any other relationships with people besides the Melameds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Once we, I remember, was some part, where my father and Mr. Kowinsky [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took some sub-contract from the exporter who sent to America shirts. And in our house, in other place-- that was 1920-something-- was big room, very big, double, like this one. And it was big table. And the material was laying out and cut later there. And so we give the subcontractor only-- wait, I says to him, my father. And this people-- the house people sew this and bring back the finished shirts. That was Mr. Kowinsky and my father. That's the shirts was sent to State. And after we-- we start here, I saw the same-- the same style, the same pattern, the same everything in United States. That was for me something [LAUGHS] new. That's not the same shirts. Still the same material, the same style.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have a lot of business with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Only it was-- business was not profitable later, after two years, something like this, was not profitable. And my father go away and bought-- partners go each separate way. That I remember. That's years ago, 1930-- '20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the 30s, could you describe what the financial circumstances of your family were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We was, you know, not the rich people, richest people in the Warsaw. Still, you know, was enough to school, pay school, live-- live, and eat, without the hunger. And we go, sometime, one time, yearly, all family to one month's vacation. That was the-- the story. And during this time, my father was working for the shipyard. That's what save big money, on this time, that later there go to business, was the money for the business. This time, my father was their [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chief guy over some, maybe, 50 people there. And I know was very good pay. That was the reason after father collect some money, started going to the business for own [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] benefit. I say so. That was 1930, something like this. This time, I was not interesting about my father's business, because I was in school. And for me, was most important the sport and not business. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have help in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Was help in the house. And all the time was something, because it was four-- four children and mother and, you know, that was all the time some help for mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the people who worked there?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is Bulka-- name. One was Anyela. [LAUGHS] Because, you know, not all the time the same person. Because young people-- all the time young people, and some other ones, you know, really not-- These two, Bulka, Anyela, Janina, that's three I remember right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what age did you finish school? At what age did you finish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I finish high school 1939. And was, you know, the very-- very tight, very different time. Because we know something going on from the west, I mean, from Germany. And in Poland, was, this time, custom-- not-- not-- not only custom, that was the regular. In other words, young fellow who finish high school and has diploma must go for two, three months for special unit military. Meantime, in high school, we was trained-- we go through military train, prepare to a reserve army officer. That's-- I go 1939, in May, after I receive, you know, the diploma, I pass examination. And I was sent to unit, where we was working physically, physically working, for two months and prepare fortification for Polish-German border. Especially, I was sent, because I already pick up the line, technical line. I was sent to a unit, where we built forts on the border, near the river on the border, forts against the tanks, German tanks. That was very interesting, construction of heavy fort, three meters thick the wall, reinforced, and so and so. And we was working there two, three months. And this time, I supposed to go to-- I supposed to be sent to army school for officers. This time was break the war. And we was-- that was the military unit, where we was. We had full equipment, with the rifle and et cetera, plus 75 kilogram other stuff, technical stuff. We was march to east. Because this part was north part of the Poland, 300 kilometer from the Warsaw, about. And we was sent to east. It was regroup the army. And was up to September 17. And this time, sometime when we march over 50 kilometers, full equipped, heavy stuff, during one night, I mean, night and day, 24 hours. That was very, very stressful and very hard stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was couple days, we walk around. Around? [LAUGHS] We go east. And September 17, our friends, Soviet, give us knife in back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Took friendship with the Hitler. Shake hands with the Hitler and divide the Poland. This time, 17 September, I was captured by Soviet-- our unit was captured by Soviet tanks during night. And we start fighting. And it was nonsense to do this, because we have only the rifles, you know, and not heavy equipment. And was only two companies, one battalion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] how, the English? I don't know. I mean, the Soviet took our rifles, our ammunition, of everything what we have, and select officer, on one side, the soldier on the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was, this time, I was not officer yet. And we was arrested in some place, where we go to the big barn. And there we was waiting for transport. Meantime, our officers, majors and captains and some rank officers, go to camp, where later there was-- after half year, shot in Katyn, where 15,000 Polish Army officer was shot by NKVD there. You know, this history tell about the Katyn. That was the-- We-- I was lucky, because, this time, I have some small money, generally speaking. And it was enough bribe the [LAUGHS] Soviet soldier-- and my watch. 20 zloty and my watch, and we go away, six guys. And because was we know-- we never like Soviet, because what the Soviet promise, never keep the promise. And we divide for three parts, and we go three ways. I took west way with my friend. One go to the east, to Vilno, and was captured later, there, by Soviet. And two went to-- because very close to Lithuania border-- go to Litva and later, there, to Sweden, and, from there, go to England and fight during the Cold War in English Navy against the Hitler. And that's the story. I come back to Warsaw, 1939. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm going through the Bug. That's big river in Poland. And standing up to the knee in the water. After it was dark, we jump, and we go through the water to Poland. Because, meantime, the Soviet and German, after shake hand and kiss each other, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put the border on the River Bug. What divided Poland, east and west, up to the Bug River took Soviet, and from the River Bug take the German. And was, you know, that divide the Poland only for one year, because two-- but then later, there start fighting each other. You know about this. And that's-- I come back to Warsaw, 1939, before Christmas. That was December. And I saw, during this walk-- a couple hundred miles, from the east to Warsaw, I go by foot. And I must go through the places where you not see the army, German army. And that was experience. Because, this time, I saw a big different, very big different between equipment and manpower, between German and Soviet and Poland either. The German was very superior equipped and was prepared to the war. And still, we was try go around the German, not go directly and face, because we was in uniform. I change couple things. For example, in the-- try be civil not a military guy. And we reach Warsaw, both with some small problems. And I saw my family in Warsaw. And, you know, that was for me very exciting.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the family circumstance change in the time that you had left and returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you see this was everything prepare the war. And Polish people was, you know, try build good feeling, be prepare for war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, that was very hard. Because Germany was big power. You know how was the powerful German Army if English, French, Belgium, Norwegian, et cetera, cannot do nothing? And Poland was the same. you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, Polish people was not prepare to do war. And probably, Poland be fighting longer, only the Soviet kill the reconstruction the army. Because we going back, back, back, I mean Polish Army. And on the east was natural cover for army, was waters and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] places where you can cover, and the trees and something like this. And from the west, only the Soviet went to Poland and kill everybody and sent right away-- arrested and sent to Siberia. That was very, very sad feeling. Thus we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thus we, you know, not like the Soviet, because these are people who trade everything and everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you got home in-- in the family situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The family situation-- you see, during the war, Warsaw was bombarded by German artillery in 1939 and also the airplane. And many, many people was without the home, because was destroyed. I found, in my family, was-- was not too big a-- an apartment. Still was maybe three or four other family, who my mother and father take and give the shelter. Thus I found the family. And my mother was all the time talking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I did everything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe someday, we-- [SOB] my children needs-- for children, in other words. [DRINKS] And after I find my family, with other friends, people, in house, you know, that's-- I mean, mother was very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thank God I come back. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after this, you know, it was a very strange situation in Warsaw. Because, you know, German-- you know, if you read the or you heard about this, Polish, Jewish, Gypsy was enemy for German people. That Hitler said. Not the nation, Hitler said. And was terror. First terror was against the Polish people. Later, there was big terror against the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know why I said, first for Polish? You see, because, right away, was a bekanntmachung, some things what was put big paper, and you must go register here. You must go there and do something what German want.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all army officer who go back from the front, supposed to be registered and supposed to be sent to Oflag. And that was first terror. Who not register, I mean, army officers, who are not in the army, because there was no Polish army, was not register, has the death sentence.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.
